name: Chronovisor Vatican
description: The Chronovisor Vatican theory posits that the Vatican possesses a secret device, the Chronovisor, capable of viewing past events. This alleged machine, supposedly developed by a team of scientists and clergy, uses electromagnetic radiation to reconstruct images and sounds from the past, allowing its operators to witness historical moments, including biblical events like the crucifixion of Jesus. The theory gained traction primarily through the claims of Father Pellegrino Ernetti, an Italian Benedictine monk and physicist, who claimed to have worked on the Chronovisor. He provided purported details about its construction and operation, and even a photograph supposedly depicting Jesus on the cross. Mainstream science dismisses the Chronovisor as pseudoscience and technologically impossible, citing the lack of verifiable evidence and the violation of known physical laws. Physicists argue that time travel, even passive observation of the past, presents insurmountable theoretical problems. Skeptics point to the photograph presented by Ernetti as being easily explainable as a manipulated image of existing artwork. The Vatican has officially denied the existence of such a device. The persistence of the Chronovisor theory highlights the enduring fascination with time travel and the secretive nature often attributed to the Vatican.
subtitle: Vatican's alleged time-viewing device

claims.evidence: Father Ernetti's testimony and purported photo.
claims.criticism: Vatican denial; lack of independent verification; photograph debunked as likely manipulated.

claims.claim: The Chronovisor can view past events.
claims.evidence: Ernetti claimed it used electromagnetic radiation to reconstruct past events.
claims.criticism: Violates known laws of physics; no scientific basis for time viewing.

claims.claim: The Chronovisor was used to witness the crucifixion of Jesus.
claims.evidence: Ernetti's claim and the supposed photo are the primary evidence.
claims.criticism: The photo is highly suspect and easily explained as a hoax; no other corroborating evidence exists.

keyFigures.name: Father Pellegrino Ernetti
keyFigures.role: Claimed to have worked on the Chronovisor and publicized its existence.

keyFigures.name: François Brune
keyFigures.role: Author who popularized Ernetti's claims in his book 'Le nouveau mystère du Vatican'.

keyFigures.name: David Icke
keyFigures.role: Prominent conspiracy theorist who incorporated the Chronovisor into his broader theories.

mythologyConnections: Akashic Records (concept of a universal record of past events)

timeline.event: Alleged development of the Chronovisor by Father Ernetti and others.
First public mention of the Chronovisor in an Italian magazine.
Publication of books and articles promoting the Chronovisor theory.
The Chronovisor theory continues to circulate online and in conspiracy circles.

sources.author: Peter Krassa
sources.work: Father Ernetti's Chronovisor: The Machine That Can See Into The Past
sources.title: Peter Krassa
